paper.title: Tokencake: A KV-Cache-centric Serving Framework for LLM-based Multi-Agent Applications

presentation_thesis: Tokencake argues that multi-agent LLM serving is bottlenecked by KV-cache management, so the runtime must manage cache lifecycle with both function-call awareness and agent criticality rather than treating memory as a passive byproduct of compute scheduling.

problem_framing.label: Workload shift
problem_framing.value: Multi-agent applications execute as DAGs with heterogeneous agent importance and frequent external function calls.

problem_framing.label: Pathology 1
problem_framing.value: Space contention causes critical inversion when non-critical agents consume scarce GPU KV-cache blocks before critical-path work arrives.

problem_framing.label: Pathology 2
problem_framing.value: Time underutilization leaves stalled agents’ KV cache idle on the GPU during long tool calls.

problem_framing.label: Why prior work misses it
problem_framing.value: Agent-aware schedulers ignore KV-cache placement, while KV-cache-centric systems remain reactive and agent-agnostic.

claims.statement: Multi-agent applications create two distinct KV-cache pathologies: critical-path space contention and function-call time underutilization.
Existing systems optimize either workflow scheduling or KV-cache management, but not both jointly for agentic workloads.
Tokencake’s central design is a KV-cache-centric runtime that exposes agent structure through the frontend and coordinates space and time schedulers around one memory pool.
The Time Scheduler should offload only when the predicted stall window exceeds transfer cost and should prefetch before the agent resumes.
The Space Scheduler avoids critical inversion by reserving memory for critical agents using hybrid priority and runtime memory-pressure feedback.
The combined policy keeps GPU memory productive and translates into large wins under load: lower end-to-end latency, higher KV-cache utilization, fewer abnormal agents, and viable offload overhead.

mechanisms.label: Frontend API
mechanisms.detail: Represents the application as a DAG, adds staged FuncNode abstractions, and lets developers provide predict_time hints.

mechanisms.label: Time Scheduler
mechanisms.detail: Uses call_start / call_finish events, predictive duration modeling, opportunistic offload, and predictive upload.

mechanisms.label: CPU block buffering
mechanisms.detail: Caches freed CPU blocks in an internal free list so bursty offload cycles avoid expensive allocator churn.

mechanisms.label: Gradual GPU reservation
mechanisms.detail: Reserves destination blocks over multiple cycles so predictive upload does not stall on one large allocation.

mechanisms.label: Space Scheduler
mechanisms.detail: Partitions GPU KV-cache into shared and reserved pools, then adjusts reserve size and per-agent shares from pressure, historical usage, and hybrid priority.

evaluation_setup.label: Implementation
evaluation_setup.value: About 9k lines of Python with Triton kernels and reused vLLM components.

evaluation_setup.label: Models / hardware
evaluation_setup.value: Qwen2.5-14B on NVIDIA A100 80GB and Qwen2.5-32B on NVIDIA H200 140GB.

evaluation_setup.label: Swap space
evaluation_setup.value: 100GB of CPU memory reserved for offloaded KV cache.

evaluation_setup.label: Workload model
evaluation_setup.value: Requests and tool latencies are both generated with Poisson processes.

evaluation_setup.label: Metrics
evaluation_setup.value: End-to-End Latency, GPU KV Cache Utilization, and Abnormal Agent Count (>1.5x mean latency for its type).

quantitative_anchors.label: Idle-cache waste
quantitative_anchors.value: Up to 18.5% of used GPU KV cache is held by stalled agents.

quantitative_anchors.label: Headline latency gain
quantitative_anchors.value: At 1.0 QPS, Tokencake reduces average end-to-end latency by over 47.06% versus vLLM.

quantitative_anchors.label: GPU utilization
quantitative_anchors.value: Tokencake sustains about 85.7% to 87.0% utilization, up to 16.9% higher than vLLM.

quantitative_anchors.label: Abnormal agents
quantitative_anchors.value: File Write abnormal agents fall from 90 to 27 versus both baselines.

quantitative_anchors.label: Transfer vs recompute
quantitative_anchors.value: For 4096 blocks, transfer costs about 60 ms while recomputation costs about 8943 ms.

quantitative_anchors.label: Mitigated upload cost
quantitative_anchors.value: At 5120 blocks, optimized upload is 4.4 ms versus 15163 ms in the unoptimized baseline.

support.detail: Comparison against Parrot, Autellix, Teola, vLLM, Mooncake, CachedAttention, LMCache
Trigger and policy differences for offload and prefetch

limitations.label: Prediction model
limitations.detail: The scheduler relies on a relatively simple execution-time predictor and could benefit from richer features such as function-call arguments.

limitations.label: Deployment scope
limitations.detail: The evaluation is single-GPU; extending the design to multi-GPU and NVLink-backed hierarchies remains future work.

notes: Use the Systems Paper Reading / OSDI-SOSP Style arc. Keep titles short and make the KV-cache thesis explicit on every evidence slide.
